reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility: phase-dependent FSN monotonicity vs reversal, and careful inference limits from a two-chemistry rupture comparison. Decoys are engineering-plausible and mostly single-fault (overgeneralization, causal misread, and scope expansion), yielding solid expert discrimination. Minor ambiguity remains because one correct option (C) could be read as over-asserting “determined by” energy content rather than merely “consistent with energy content being a differentiator,” slightly softening boundary tightness and discrimination.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware admissibility: TRonset is a valid but incomplete hazard signal, and universalizing it across fire-safety contexts is an overreach given energy-content-driven rupture and chemistry-specific failure modes. The prompt supplies enough contrasting observations (rupture vs non-rupture; TRonset trend; identical conditions) to force experts to articulate the governing boundaries. Decoy assessment is limited because it is short-answer with no explicit options, slightly reducing single-fault diagnostics and making grading more rubric-dependent.
